title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a fashion designer called Maya Sterling talking about her work with sustainable textiles. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a fashion designer called Maya Sterling talking about her work with sustainable textiles. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Maya Sterling discusses the revolution of eco-friendly fabrics in the fashion industry.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. My name is Maya Sterling. I’ve been in the fashion industry for twenty years, but it was only five years ago that I decided to completely change my approach. I realized that the way we produce clothes is simply not sustainable. [7]I first became interested in alternative materials after visiting a research lab that focused on mycelium[/7], which is the root structure of mushrooms. It was a turning point for me. Mycelium can be grown into a leather-like material that is completely biodegradable. One of the biggest hurdles was making the material durable enough for high-end fashion. [8]I found that the most difficult part of the development phase was achieving a consistent texture[/8], as natural growth can be quite unpredictable. We also experimented with agricultural waste. [9]The use of pineapple leaves to create a durable textile was a breakthrough for our summer collection[/9]. It’s lightweight and incredibly strong. Regarding the manufacturing process, we try to keep everything local. [10]We decided to move our production to a small family-owned factory in Scotland[/10] to reduce our carbon footprint. It was a risky move, but it paid off in terms of quality control. The workspace is very different from what I was used to; [11]I was pleasantly surprised to find that the quiet atmosphere of the studio helped me focus[/11] much better than in a busy city office. When we launched our first collection, the feedback was overwhelming. [12]Seeing our designs featured in a documentary about environmental change was a proud moment[/12] for everyone involved. I truly believe that the future of fashion lies in biotechnology. [13]I am convinced that consumer education is the most powerful tool we have[/13] to drive the industry forward. People need to know where their clothes come from. As we say in the studio, [14]we are not just making clothes, we are 'stitching a future' that respects the planet[/14]. It’s a long journey, but every garment counts.

dialogue.speaker: maya
dialogue.text: Hello. My name is Maya Sterling. I’ve been in the fashion industry for twenty years, but it was only five years ago that I decided to completely change my approach. I realized that the way we produce clothes is simply not sustainable. I first became interested in alternative materials after visiting a research lab that focused on mycelium, which is the root structure of mushrooms. It was a turning point for me. Mycelium can be grown into a leather-like material that is completely biodegradable. One of the biggest hurdles was making the material durable enough for high-end fashion. I found that the most difficult part of the development phase was achieving a consistent texture, as natural growth can be quite unpredictable. We also experimented with agricultural waste. The use of pineapple leaves to create a durable textile was a breakthrough for our summer collection. It’s lightweight and incredibly strong. Regarding the manufacturing process, we try to keep everything local. We decided to move our production to a small family-owned factory in Scotland to reduce our carbon footprint. It was a risky move, but it paid off in terms of quality control. The workspace is very different from what I was used to; I was pleasantly surprised to find that the quiet atmosphere of the studio helped me focus much better than in a busy city office. When we launched our first collection, the feedback was overwhelming. Seeing our designs featured in a documentary about environmental change was a proud moment for everyone involved. I truly believe that the future of fashion lies in biotechnology. I am convinced that consumer education is the most powerful tool we have to drive the industry forward. People need to know where their clothes come from. As we say in the studio, we are not just making clothes, we are 'stitching a future' that respects the planet. It’s a long journey, but every garment counts.

questions.question: Maya's interest in sustainable fashion began when she saw how (7) could be used as a textile.
questions.explanation: She mentions visiting a lab focused on mycelium, which was a turning point.

questions.question: Maya mentions that getting the (8) right was the biggest challenge during the testing period.
questions.explanation: She states that achieving a consistent texture was the most difficult part.

questions.question: The discovery that (9) could be turned into fabric allowed Maya to expand her summer range.
questions.explanation: Using pineapple leaves was a breakthrough for her summer collection.

questions.question: Maya chose to base her manufacturing in (10) to help lower the company's impact on the environment.
questions.explanation: She moved production to a factory in Scotland to reduce the carbon footprint.

questions.question: Maya found that the (11) in her new workspace improved her ability to work.
questions.explanation: She was surprised that the quiet atmosphere helped her focus.

questions.question: Maya felt a sense of achievement when her work appeared in a (12) .
questions.explanation: Being featured in a documentary about environmental change was a proud moment.

questions.question: According to Maya, the best way to change the fashion industry is through (13) .
questions.explanation: She believes consumer education is the most powerful tool.

questions.question: The motto used by Maya's team is that they are (14) for the planet.
questions.explanation: She uses the specific phrase 'stitching a future' as their motto.
